name: Aurora Files
summary: Open-source self-hosted private cloud storage and file-sharing platform with web, mobile, desktop sync, public links, WebDAV, S3-backed storage, and optional browser-side encryption.

account.benefits: Users need an account on the deployed Aurora Files instance to manage files; external contacts can receive secure file and folder links.

developer.notes: Aurora Files is developed on GitHub, can use S3-compatible storage, and exposes WebDAV-style access for compatible clients; automation still depends on the operator's deployment.

allowed_file_types.notes: Aurora Files is a self-hosted storage platform; accepted file types, quotas, and enforcement depend on the instance configuration and backend storage.

public_sharing.notes: The official page says users can share files and folders with teammates, teams, and external contacts via secure links.

security.notes: Transport policy is deployment-dependent for self-hosted instances. Aurora Files also advertises browser-side AES-256 encryption where the server does not receive encryption keys.

sources.label: Aurora Files product page
sources.notes: Official product page describes Aurora Files as open-source private cloud storage for teams and personal users, with sharing, secure links, web/mobile/desktop access, browser-side AES-256 encryption, and S3-compatible storage support.

sources.label: Aurora Files GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official source repository identifies Aurora Files as an AGPLv3 open-source file storage platform.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing Aurora Files under file sharing.

primary_use.notes: Aurora Files is a self-hosted file storage and sharing platform rather than a public file-hosting service.

sharing_surface.value: User/team shares and secure external links
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing happens through instance accounts, team spaces, and secure file or folder links for external contacts.

max_file_size.notes: No single public hosted limit applies; upload size and storage quotas depend on the instance and backend configuration.

persistence_model.notes: Files persist according to the deploying operator's storage, account, and retention policies.

storage_model.notes: Aurora Files can store data locally or on S3-compatible storage.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth depends on the server, network, and storage backend chosen by the operator.

name: Garage
summary: Open-source S3-compatible object store designed for self-hosted, resilient storage across modest machines, with CLI/API workflows and static website/object delivery options.

account.benefits: Operators create buckets and access keys; end-user access depends on S3 credentials, public bucket policy, website hosting, or application integration.

developer.notes: Garage implements the Amazon S3 API and the quick start documents AWS CLI uploads/downloads, bucket creation, keys, and the Garage administration CLI.

allowed_file_types.notes: Garage stores S3 objects rather than applying a normal consumer file-host extension policy; object type and sharing policy depend on bucket/application configuration.

public_sharing.notes: Garage supports S3-compatible object access and static website hosting, but public delivery must be configured by the operator.

security.notes: Transport and encryption are deployment-dependent. The quick start uses local HTTP examples, while production deployments normally sit behind operator-controlled TLS/reverse-proxy configuration.

sources.label: Garage homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage describes Garage as an S3 object store, focused on reliability outside datacenters, redundancy, low requirements, and S3 compatibility.

sources.label: Garage quick start
sources.notes: Official quick start documents single-node deployment, bucket/key creation, AWS CLI uploads and downloads, and Garage administration commands.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing Garage under S3-compatible file servers.

primary_use.notes: Garage is an object store for operators and developers rather than a public consumer file host.

sharing_surface.value: Buckets, S3 clients, presigned/public object access, and static sites
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing happens through S3-compatible object access, application integrations, or configured public/static website delivery.

max_file_size.notes: No hosted per-file limit applies; practical limits depend on S3 compatibility, node capacity, and deployment configuration.

persistence_model.notes: Objects persist according to bucket configuration, cluster health, and operator retention/deletion policies.

storage_model.notes: Garage stores data across operator-managed nodes and is designed for replication across zones.

bandwidth_model.notes: Throughput and egress depend on operator hardware, network, reverse proxy, and S3 client/application usage.

name: LinShare
summary: Open-source secure file-sharing platform for organizations, with on-premises deployment, shared spaces, guest accounts, quotas, upload-size controls, and governance features.

account.benefits: LinShare is account and organization oriented; administrators can create guest accounts for temporary upload/download access.

developer.notes: The official page links to GitHub and positions LinShare as open source, but this row does not verify a simple public upload API or command-line file-host flow.

allowed_file_types.notes: LinShare administrators can define file-size and extension filtering rules by domain or user scope.

public_sharing.notes: LinShare supports shared spaces and guest accounts for external upload/download workflows, subject to administrator policy.

security.notes: LinShare promotes TLS protection and governance features, but transport and storage encryption depend on the deployed instance and operator policy.

sources.label: LinShare product page
sources.notes: Official page describes LinShare as an open-source file-sharing platform with shared spaces, guest accounts, quota assignment, file-size and extension filtering, on-premises deployment, and GPL licensing.

sources.label: LinShare core GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official source repository for the LinShare core project.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing LinShare under file sharing.

primary_use.notes: LinShare targets businesses, governments, and organizations that want a governed self-hosted file-sharing platform.

sharing_surface.value: Shared spaces, guest accounts, and managed uploads/downloads
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing is built around managed users, guests, shared spaces, and administrator-defined policies.

max_file_size.notes: Maximum upload size is configurable by administrators and can vary by domain, user, or account policy.

persistence_model.notes: Retention and deletion depend on administrator policy, shared-space rules, and the deployed storage backend.

storage_model.notes: The official page offers on-premises deployment and cloud/contact-us options rather than one fixed public storage tier.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth and transfer performance depend on the operator's infrastructure and policy.

name: Nextcloud
summary: Open-source self-hosted cloud storage and file-sharing platform with public links, file drop, federated shares, WebDAV/client APIs, and optional hosted providers.

account.benefits: Users normally need an account on a Nextcloud instance or provider to manage files; public links and File Drop can expose selected files or folders to external recipients.

developer.notes: Nextcloud publishes client APIs, OCS share APIs, and WebDAV APIs; CLI friendliness is generally through WebDAV/rclone/curl-style clients and instance credentials.

allowed_file_types.notes: Nextcloud stores and shares files on a chosen server/provider; accepted file types, upload limits, and quota enforcement depend on instance configuration.

public_sharing.notes: Official docs describe public link shares, File Drop, internal shares, and federated shares with other Nextcloud or ownCloud servers.

security.notes: Security posture is instance-dependent. Nextcloud supports controlled sharing and encryption mechanisms, but end-to-end encryption is not the default behavior for all file sharing.

sources.label: Nextcloud Files product page
sources.notes: Official page describes Nextcloud Files as secure cloud storage and file-sharing software for sync, sharing, and collaboration.

sources.label: Nextcloud file-sharing docs
sources.notes: Official user manual documents public link shares, File Drop, password protection, expiration dates, internal shares, and federation.

sources.label: Nextcloud server GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official source repository describes Nextcloud Server as a place to store, sync, and share data, with AGPL licensing.

sources.label: Nextcloud client APIs
sources.notes: Official developer manual lists client APIs including OCS share APIs and WebDAV.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing Nextcloud under file sharing.

primary_use.notes: Nextcloud is a full cloud storage, sync, and collaboration platform rather than a single public file host.

sharing_surface.value: Public links, File Drop, internal shares, and federated shares
sharing_surface.notes: Nextcloud sharing includes public links, upload-only File Drop, internal account/group shares, and federation with compatible servers.

max_file_size.notes: No universal public limit applies; limits are configured by the selected instance/provider and server environment.

persistence_model.notes: Files persist according to the instance/provider storage policy and user/account deletion choices.

storage_model.value: Self-hosted server or hosted provider instance
storage_model.notes: Nextcloud can be installed by the user or used through a provider listed by Nextcloud.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth depends on the server, provider, and configured apps/infrastructure.

name: ownCloud
summary: Open-source file sync and share platform with public links, guest and federated sharing, WebDAV APIs, desktop/mobile clients, and self-hosted or SaaS deployment options.

account.benefits: Users need an account on an ownCloud instance or SaaS workspace to manage files; public links and guest features can expose selected items externally.

developer.notes: ownCloud Infinite Scale exposes HTTP APIs including WebDAV, and its GitHub README documents Docker-based self-hosting.

allowed_file_types.notes: ownCloud is deployed per organization or SaaS workspace, so file-type policy, quotas, and upload limits depend on the selected instance and administrator policy.

public_sharing.notes: Official docs and product pages describe public links for files/folders, password and expiry controls, drop folders, guest users, and federated sharing.

security.notes: Security posture is instance-dependent. ownCloud emphasizes controlled sharing and data sovereignty, but transport and encryption details depend on deployment and edition configuration.

sources.label: ownCloud product page
sources.notes: Official page describes ownCloud as a secure file-sharing and real-time collaboration platform with public links and SaaS/self-hosted options.

sources.label: ownCloud public link docs
sources.notes: Official docs describe public link shares, drop folders, passwords, expiry dates, and permissions.

sources.label: ownCloud Infinite Scale GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official repository describes ownCloud Infinite Scale as the new file sync and share platform and includes Docker quickstart instructions.

sources.label: ownCloud APIs
sources.notes: Official developer documentation describes ownCloud HTTP APIs including WebDAV and OpenAPI-backed services.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing ownCloud under file sharing.

primary_use.value: Self-hosted or SaaS file sync and share
primary_use.notes: ownCloud is a full EFSS/cloud collaboration platform rather than a single public upload host.

sharing_surface.value: Public links, guest users, drop folders, and federated shares
sharing_surface.notes: ownCloud sharing can include public links, guest users, upload-only drop folders, and federation.

max_file_size.notes: No universal public limit applies; limits depend on ownCloud edition, deployment, and administrator configuration.

persistence_model.notes: Files persist according to the deployed instance or SaaS workspace policy and user/account deletion choices.

storage_model.value: Self-hosted ownCloud instance or ownCloud.online SaaS
storage_model.notes: ownCloud can run on operator infrastructure or through ownCloud.online.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth depends on the server, provider, network, and deployment policy.

name: Pixelfed
summary: Decentralized, open-source photo-sharing social network that can be used as an account-based alternative way to publish and share images across the Fediverse.

account.benefits: Users need an account on a Pixelfed instance to publish posts; limits and moderation depend on the chosen instance.

developer.notes: Pixelfed is open-source ActivityPub software. API and upload behavior are instance/application oriented rather than a simple anonymous file-host API.

allowed_file_types.notes: Pixelfed is photo/video social publishing software; upload limits and accepted media types are configured by each instance.

public_sharing.notes: Pixelfed posts and albums are designed for public/federated photo sharing, subject to post privacy and instance settings.

security.notes: Pixelfed instances run over HTTPS, but public/federated posts are not positioned as end-to-end encrypted file storage.

sources.label: Pixelfed.social about page
sources.notes: Official Pixelfed.social about page describes photo sharing, albums, comments, collections, and sign-up on an instance.

sources.label: Pixelfed GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official source repository describes Pixelfed as photo sharing for the fediverse and AGPL-licensed open-source software.

sources.label: Pixelfed docs prerequisites
sources.notes: Official docs show upload/file limits are configured at the instance/server level.

sources.label: FMHY Image Hosts
sources.notes: Seed list described Pixelfed as decentralized image sharing that requires sign-up; official sources support that classification.

primary_use.notes: Pixelfed is primarily a decentralized social network for photo/video posts rather than a normal anonymous file-host.

sharing_surface.value: Posts, albums, profiles, and ActivityPub federation
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing happens through posts, albums, profiles, and federated social distribution across instances.

max_file_size.notes: Upload size depends on the Pixelfed instance and its server configuration; official docs reference instance/server upload settings.

persistence_model.notes: Posts persist according to the selected instance's policies and the user's account/post deletion choices.

storage_model.notes: Storage is provided by each Pixelfed instance, not by a single central file-host pool.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth and delivery behavior depend on the user's selected instance and federated delivery paths.

name: ProjectSend
summary: Free open-source self-hosted client file-sharing portal with managed users, client groups, upload/download permissions, download limits, auto-expiration, and server-side encryption at rest.

account.benefits: ProjectSend is built around administrator-managed users and clients; clients can receive assigned files and upload files back according to permissions.

developer.notes: ProjectSend publishes source/download/docs links, but this row does not verify a public upload API or command-line file-host flow.

allowed_file_types.notes: ProjectSend is self-hosted and permission-based; accepted file types, upload size, quotas, and expiration behavior depend on administrator configuration and server limits.

public_sharing.notes: ProjectSend is designed to share files securely with clients and allow clients to upload files back, though it is a client portal rather than an anonymous public file host.

security.notes: Transport security is deployment-dependent. The official feature list advertises AES-256-GCM server-side encryption for files at rest.

sources.label: ProjectSend homepage
sources.notes: Official page describes ProjectSend as free open-source self-hosted file sharing with client-specific access, users/clients/groups, client uploads, download limits, disk quota management, auto-expiration, S3 support, and AES-256-GCM encryption at rest.

sources.label: ProjectSend documentation
sources.notes: Official documentation entry point linked from the ProjectSend homepage.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing ProjectSend under file sharing.

primary_use.notes: ProjectSend is focused on controlled client file exchange rather than anonymous public hosting.

sharing_surface.value: Client accounts, assigned files, upload-back flows, and managed permissions
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing happens through administrator-managed users, clients, groups, roles, and file permissions.

max_file_size.notes: No universal public hosted limit applies; practical limits depend on PHP/server configuration and administrator policy.

persistence_model.notes: ProjectSend supports auto-expiration and download limits; file lifetime depends on administrator/user configuration.

storage_model.value: Self-hosted server storage with optional external/S3 destinations
storage_model.notes: ProjectSend runs on the operator's server and advertises S3 support and upload destination controls.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth depends on the web server, storage backend, and operator hosting plan.

name: Pydio Cells
summary: Open-core self-hosted document sharing and collaboration platform with workspaces/cells, secure file/folder sharing, public links, REST APIs, S3-compatible file-content access, and WebDAV.

account.benefits: Users need an account on a Pydio Cells deployment to manage workspaces and files; public links can expose selected resources externally.

developer.notes: Pydio Cells publishes REST APIs, an S3-compatible API for file contents, and WebDAV access, so automation is possible with compatible clients and instance credentials.

allowed_file_types.notes: Pydio Cells is deployed per organization; accepted file types, quota, and upload policy depend on workspace, datasource, and administrator configuration.

public_sharing.notes: Official docs describe public links and Cells/folder sharing for internal and external collaborators.

security.notes: Transport and storage security depend on deployment. Pydio positions Cells for compliance and secure document sharing, but this row does not treat end-to-end encryption as the default behavior.

sources.label: Pydio Cells overview
sources.notes: Official overview describes Pydio Cells as an open-core self-hosted document sharing, collaboration, and management platform with workspaces/cells and secure file/folder sharing.

sources.label: Pydio sharing features docs
sources.notes: Official docs describe public links, permissions, access restrictions, and sharing with users through Cells.

sources.label: Pydio open-source page
sources.notes: Official community page describes Pydio's open-source/open-core positioning.

sources.label: Pydio main APIs
sources.notes: Official developer docs describe REST APIs, S3-compatible file-content access, and WebDAV/other APIs.

sources.label: Pydio REST API reference
sources.notes: Official generated REST API reference lists service endpoints including Share Service.

sources.label: Pydio Share Service API
sources.notes: Official API docs describe CRUD operations for Cells and public links.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing Pydio under file sharing.

primary_use.notes: Pydio Cells is a self-hosted document sharing and collaboration platform rather than a public anonymous file host.

sharing_surface.value: Workspaces, Cells, public links, and collaborator shares
sharing_surface.notes: Sharing is built around workspaces/cells, public links, permissions, and collaborator access.

max_file_size.notes: No universal public hosted limit applies; practical limits depend on deployment, datasource, and administrator policy.

persistence_model.notes: Persistence depends on the deployed storage datasource, workspace policy, and administrator/user actions.

storage_model.value: Self-hosted datasources with filesystem, S3-compatible, and WebDAV-accessible paths
storage_model.notes: Pydio Cells can connect to datasources and exposes file access through web UI, APIs, S3-compatible access, and WebDAV.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth depends on the organization's server, datasource, network, and policy.

name: Seafile
summary: Open-source file sync, cloud storage, and collaboration platform with public share links, upload links, granular permissions, Web APIs, WebDAV-related components, and optional client-side library encryption.

account.benefits: Users need an account on a Seafile server/provider to manage libraries; public share links and upload links can give outside users access without a Seafile account.

developer.notes: Seafile publishes Web API documentation and source repositories; CLI friendliness is generally through Web API/WebDAV/sync clients rather than a simple anonymous upload endpoint.

allowed_file_types.notes: Seafile stores files in libraries on a chosen server/provider; accepted file types, quotas, and upload-size limits depend on deployment and administrator policy.

public_sharing.notes: Official docs describe public share links for files/folders and upload links for folders that can be used without recipient login.

security.notes: Transport security depends on the deployment. Seafile's source docs describe encrypted libraries and client-side encryption, but encryption is a library/user choice rather than universal for all shares.

sources.label: Seafile homepage
sources.notes: Official homepage describes Seafile as file sync/share/cloud storage software with sharing permissions, password protection, expiration time, and access controls for share links.

sources.label: Seafile sharing docs
sources.notes: Official user manual describes public share links, upload links, internal links, permissions, passwords, and expiration times.

sources.label: Seafile GitHub repository
sources.notes: Official source repository describes Seafile as open-source cloud storage with file sharing, password-protected download links, upload links, groups, and client-side encryption.

sources.label: Seafile Web API docs
sources.notes: Official admin manual points to current Seafile Web API documentation.

sources.label: Awesome Storage seed
sources.notes: Discovery seed listing Seafile under file sharing.

primary_use.value: Self-hosted or provider-hosted file sync and share
primary_use.notes: Seafile is a file sync/share and collaboration platform rather than a single public anonymous file host.

sharing_surface.value: Share links, upload links, libraries, groups, and internal links
sharing_surface.notes: Seafile can share files/folders with public links, upload-only links, users, groups, organizations, and internal links.

max_file_size.notes: No universal hosted limit applies; limits depend on the chosen server/provider and administrator configuration.

persistence_model.notes: Files persist in Seafile libraries according to server/provider storage, retention, and user deletion policies.

storage_model.notes: Seafile organizes stored files into libraries on a Seafile server/provider, with sync clients and web access.

bandwidth_model.notes: Bandwidth depends on the server, provider, clients, and administrator policy.

name: SpectrShare
summary: Browser-to-browser P2P sharing method with no server storage, no account, no per-file size limit, WebRTC encryption, and optional in-browser video conversion.

account.benefits: No accounts are used; sharing is link/QR based while the host tab remains open.

developer.notes: SpectrShare is browser/P2P oriented and does not expose a public upload API. A previously cited GitHub repository link now returns 404, so open-source status is no longer treated as verified.

allowed_file_types.notes: All files can be downloaded; many image, audio, video, document, spreadsheet, email, ebook, and code formats can also be previewed in-browser.

public_sharing.notes: Sharing creates a link or QR code for recipients while the host tab remains open.

security.notes: The FAQ and technical notes describe direct WebRTC transfers with DTLS/end-to-end encryption and no server-side file storage.

sources.label: FAQ
sources.notes: FAQ describes P2P browser file sharing, no accounts, no server storage, no per-file size limit, supported preview formats, and WebRTC encryption.

sources.label: Technical details
sources.notes: Technical article explains direct WebRTC DataChannel transfer, streaming reads, ephemeral behavior, and DTLS encryption.

sources.label: FMHY video file hosts seed
sources.notes: Used only as the discovery seed for this batch; host facts are verified against first-party pages where available.

primary_use.notes: SpectrShare is a live transfer tool rather than a cloud file host.

sharing_surface.value: Share link or QR code while the host browser tab stays open
sharing_surface.notes: Recipients can download through the generated link/QR code during the live sharing session.

max_file_size.notes: The FAQ says there is no per-file size limit, with practical browser/device memory constraints for some fallback paths.

persistence_model.notes: Files exist for the transfer session only and are gone when the host tab closes.

storage_model.notes: Files are transferred directly between browsers and are not stored on SpectrShare servers.

bandwidth_model.notes: Throughput depends on host upload bandwidth, recipient download bandwidth, device capacity, and recipient count.

name: Telegram
summary: Cloud-based messaging service that can double as an ad hoc file-sharing method through chat attachments, channels, and public links.

account.benefits: A Telegram account is required. Shared files sync across devices and can be distributed through chats, channels, or public links.

developer.notes: Telegram publishes official file-upload and download API docs, but the consumer sharing workflow is chat-centric rather than a normal CLI file-host flow.

allowed_file_types.notes: Telegram says users can send and receive files of any type up to 2 GB each in regular cloud chats.

security.notes: Regular Telegram cloud chats are not end-to-end encrypted. Telegram treats Secret Chats as a separate, device-specific secure mode.

sources.label: Telegram FAQ
sources.notes: Official FAQ describing Telegram as cloud-based, supporting unlimited sharing of files up to 2 GB each, with Secret Chats as a separate privacy mode.

sources.label: Telegram 2 GB file sharing announcement
sources.notes: Official blog post announcing unlimited file sharing with files up to 2 GB each.

sources.label: Telegram downloads and attachments update
sources.notes: Official update saying users can send files of any type up to 2 GB each and access them from any device with unlimited cloud storage.

sources.label: Telegram file API docs
sources.notes: Official developer documentation for uploading and downloading files through Telegram APIs.

sources.label: Telegram Premium FAQ
sources.notes: Official Premium FAQ listing faster download speed as a paid feature and confirming unlimited cloud storage remains free.

sources.label: Telegram CDN docs
sources.notes: Official infrastructure documentation explaining that popular public files may be delivered from CDN data centers depending on regional traffic and popularity.

primary_use.notes: Telegram is primarily a cloud-based messaging platform that doubles as an ad hoc file-sharing method through conversations, channels, and public posts.

sharing_surface.notes: Shared files can move through private chats, groups, channels, and publicly reachable links.

max_file_size.notes: Telegram says users can send files of any type up to 2 GB each in regular cloud chats.

persistence_model.notes: Files in regular cloud chats remain until deleted by the user or chat participants. Secret Chats are separate and can self-destruct.

storage_model.notes: Telegram positions regular chats as unlimited cloud storage for shared files and media.

bandwidth_model.notes: Conditional: Telegram does not publish a simple file-host-style bandwidth quota. The official Premium FAQ makes faster download speed a paid feature, and Telegram's CDN docs show public-file delivery can depend on infrastructure path and popularity.

name: VKontakte
summary: Social network whose document attachments can also be used as an ad hoc file-sharing method.

account.benefits: A VK account is required to use document attachments as a sharing method inside the social network.

developer.notes: VK has an official developer platform, but this seed entry has not yet mapped a dedicated document-upload workflow in the public docs.

allowed_file_types.notes: Official VK document-upload documentation says document uploads may be any format except MP3 and executable files.

security.notes: VK runs over HTTPS, but the public official surfaces used here do not position document attachments as end-to-end encrypted file storage.

sources.label: VKontakte homepage
sources.notes: Official VK homepage confirming the service is an active social network product.

sources.label: VK developer portal
sources.notes: Official VK developer portal confirming an API surface exists.

sources.label: VK document upload documentation
sources.notes: Official VK developer documentation for document uploads states that documents may be any format except MP3 and executable files, with a file size of no more than 200 MB.

primary_use.notes: VK document attachments are secondary to the platform's main social-network workflow.

sharing_surface.value: Document attachments in chats and social surfaces
sharing_surface.notes: Files are shared through VK's account-based social and messaging surfaces rather than through a dedicated file-host workflow.

max_file_size.notes: Official VK document-upload documentation states that uploaded documents must be no more than 200 MB.

persistence_model.notes: Conditional: VK is a social-network document attachment surface rather than a standalone file host; the accessible official surfaces do not publish a fixed file-host-style retention window.

storage_model.notes: Conditional: storage is tied to VK account and document-attachment flows; the accessible official surfaces do not publish a separate file-host-style storage quota.

bandwidth_model.notes: Conditional: file access is delivered through VK's social and messaging surfaces; the accessible official surfaces do not publish a standalone file-host bandwidth cap.
